title: Best Almond Butter 2026: 5 Jars Ranked by Ingredients and Taste
description: Natural, no-stir, organic, raw — tested picks for daily eating, baking, and sports nutrition.
lede: Almond butter is one of those products where the ingredient list tells most of the story. The best jars have one item: almonds. Everything added after that — palm oil, sugar, salt, emulsifiers — exists to improve shelf life, texture, or to compensate for cheaper processing. These five cover the full range from single-ingredient raw to convenient no-stir, each with a specific use case.

sections.heading: How to Choose Almond Butter: Natural vs. No-Stir and Everything Between
paragraphs: Almond butter buying decisions come down to ingredient philosophy, texture preference, and how much you care about convenience versus purity.

subsections.heading: Ingredient Count
paragraphs: One ingredient (almonds) means you're getting the most nutritionally dense, additive-free product. Each additional ingredient — palm oil, sunflower oil, sugar, salt, emulsifiers — trades off some nutritional purity for convenience or flavor. Neither approach is wrong, but knowing what you're paying for matters.

subsections.heading: Roasted vs. Raw
paragraphs: Roasted almond butter has the familiar toasted, nutty flavor most people expect. Raw almond butter (never heated above 115°F) has a milder, slightly sweeter taste and higher enzyme activity. Raw costs more and has a shorter shelf life — it's primarily for raw food nutrition enthusiasts.

subsections.heading: Natural vs. No-Stir
paragraphs: Natural almond butters separate — the oil floats to the top. This is normal. You stir it back in once, store upside down between uses, and the problem is minor. No-stir versions use emulsifiers or partially hydrogenated oil to prevent separation. Convenient, but read the label to see what they added.

subsections.heading: Blanched vs. Skin-On
paragraphs: Skin-on almond butter (the most common type) has a slightly more robust, nutty flavor with a bit of bitterness from the skins. Blanched almond butter is smoother, milder, and lighter in color. Blanched works better in recipes where a cleaner flavor is needed.

subsections.heading: Palm Oil
paragraphs: Palm oil is added to prevent separation without affecting flavor much. The sustainability concern is deforestation — look for RSPO-certified palm oil if this matters to you, or choose palm oil-free options like Wild Friends. Sunflower oil is the most common alternative.

sections.heading: Bottom line
paragraphs: Justin's is the practical anchor — the two-ingredient formula threads the convenience-versus-purity needle perfectly for daily use. Barney Bare is the choice when texture matters most. Artisana is the answer for raw food protocols. For households that just want to open a jar and spread without thinking, MaraNatha delivers that experience reliably.

faqs.q: Is almond butter healthier than peanut butter?
faqs.a: Marginally, for most metrics. Almond butter has more vitamin E, magnesium, and fiber. Peanut butter has slightly more protein. Both are calorie-dense (around 100 calories per tablespoon) and excellent sources of healthy fats. The difference is minor — choose the one you'll actually eat.

Natural almond butter separates because it contains no emulsifiers — the oils are lighter than the almond paste and float to the top over time. This is a sign of quality, not a defect. Stir once when you open the jar, then store upside down between uses to minimize separation.

faqs.q: How much almond butter per day is healthy?
faqs.a: Most nutritionists point to 1–2 tablespoons (16–32g) as a useful daily serving. At that amount you get roughly 7–14g of healthy fats, 3–6g of protein, and 1–2g of fiber without overshooting calories. More than 4 tablespoons per day can add a significant caloric load for most adults.

faqs.q: What's the best almond butter for baking?
faqs.a: For baking, use a no-stir variety like MaraNatha or Justin's — consistent texture means predictable results in cookies and bars. Natural almond butters with oil separation can make baked goods greasy if the oil isn't fully incorporated. Avoid flavored versions in recipes.

faqs.q: Does almond butter need to be refrigerated?
faqs.a: After opening, refrigeration extends shelf life from 1–2 months (pantry) to 3–4 months. It's not required for safety, but the oils in natural almond butter can go rancid faster at room temperature, especially in a warm kitchen. No-stir varieties keep longer unrefrigerated due to emulsifiers.

products.review: Justin's earns its bestseller status by threading the needle between natural and convenient. Two ingredients — dry-roasted almonds and a small amount of palm oil — give it the no-stir consistency most people want without the long list of additives found in supermarket brands. The roast flavor is well-balanced: nutty without being too intense. Available in squeeze packs, which is genuinely useful for pre-workout portability or travel.
pros: Two ingredients — dry-roasted almonds and palm oil
Widely available at mainstream grocery prices
cons: Contains palm oil — sustainability concern for some
Less intense almond flavor than raw options

products.review: Barney's blanching approach is distinctive — removing the almond skin before grinding produces a noticeably smoother, milder paste than skin-on almond butters. The single ingredient is blanched almonds, full stop. The flavor is cleaner and less intense than skin-on varieties, making it more versatile in recipes where you want almond flavor without the slight bitterness from the skins. Requires minimal stirring.
pros: Blanched almonds produce noticeably smoother texture
Mild flavor works in both sweet and savory dishes

products.review: Wild Friends avoids palm oil — the texture stays creamy through stone-grinding rather than emulsification. The result is lighter than standard jar butters with a slight natural sweetness. The Oregon-based brand sources non-GMO almonds and keeps the ingredient list to almonds, sunflower oil, and sea salt. If palm oil sourcing is a concern, this is the cleaner alternative to Justin's at a similar price point.
pros: No palm oil — stone-ground for natural creaminess

products.review: The key distinction here is raw — the almonds are not roasted before grinding, which preserves a higher nutrient density and produces a distinctly different flavor profile. Raw almond butter has an almost sweet, grassy character compared to the toasted nuttiness of roasted versions. Single ingredient, certified organic, thicker consistency that needs stirring. For people prioritizing raw food nutrition, this is the standout option.
pros: Raw almonds preserve maximum nutrient density

products.review: MaraNatha's emulsification formula prevents oil separation permanently — open the jar, use it, close it. This is the practical daily-driver for households where natural almond butters with their oil separation get abandoned in the back of the pantry. The flavor is solid: mild roasted almond with light salt and a touch of sugar. Not for purists, but for consistent, hassle-free daily use, it performs exactly as advertised.
pros: Consistent texture from first to last spoonful
cons: Added oils and salt in the formula
Less pure ingredient profile than natural options

methodology: We assessed each product on flavor profile, sourcing transparency, value per serving, packaging integrity, and how well it performed across common use cases. Documented certifications and verified user reviews were cross-checked against marketing claims.
